scenes.title: import defer
scenes.tagline: Bun PR #30975 — Stage 3 deferred module evaluation

beats.narration: A pull request against Bun -- the all-in-one JavaScript runtime. Pull request thirty-thousand-nine-seventy-five.
It implements import defer -- a Stage 3 proposal for deferred module evaluation. A deferred module is fetched and linked, but its body does not run until the first time you touch it.
Fifteen files, a few hundred lines. The interesting part is not the size -- it is the threading.

JavaScriptCore -- the engine Bun embeds -- already shipped the runtime machinery for import defer. So this pull request's job is plumbing: threading one new flag, end to end, until the engine actually receives it.
It begins at the parser, which learns to read defer as a phase keyword. The intent is then recorded on the syntax tree node, and on the import record.
The printer carries it into the module's wire format. The Rust-to-C-plus-plus bridge passes it across the language boundary.
And the engine option, useImportDefer, is switched on -- so JavaScriptCore finally honors the deferral the developer asked for.

scenes.heading: A keyword that breaks nothing

beats.narration: The hard part of this change is not the feature -- it is not breaking the language. defer is not a reserved word; import defer from a module must keep parsing as an ordinary default import.
So the parser treats defer as a phase keyword only when the very next token is an asterisk -- the namespace star. Anything else, and defer is simply an identifier again.
And the comparison is on the raw token bytes -- so an escaped or obfuscated spelling of defer cannot slip through and be mistaken for the keyword.
When it does match, the import is built with phase underscore defer set true. That single boolean is what the entire rest of the compiler now has to carry.

scenes.heading: Re-keying the module table

beats.narration: The deferral must survive into the module's wire format -- the table of requested modules the printer hands to the engine. Before, that table was keyed by the import specifier, alone.
After, it is keyed by a pair -- specifier and phase -- because the same module can now be imported twice: once eagerly, once deferred.
A parallel array of phase bytes is serialized alongside the keys, mirroring how JavaScriptCore itself records its requested modules.
And because this alters the on-disk shape of Bun's transpiler cache, the cache version is bumped -- so stale entries are cleanly invalidated, never misread.
What else does it touch? The transpiler cache gains a phase array, so its format version is bumped -- twenty to twenty-one -- and older on-disk caches are cleanly invalidated.
The dead-code-elimination pass learns a new rule: never strip an unused deferred namespace binding. Dropping it would silently re-eagerize the very module the developer asked to defer.
Five C functions in the JSC bridge gain a trailing phase argument -- an A-B-I change, threaded carefully through the Rust wrappers and the C-plus-plus side.

And one honest, documented limitation: when the bundler inlines a deferred dependency into the same chunk, defer semantics are lost. Only external imports preserve it -- a known edge, declared, not hidden.

scenes.heading: PR #30975, in four ideas
points: `defer` becomes a phase keyword — but only when followed by `*`.
One `phase_defer` flag threads from parser to engine.
The requested-modules table is re-keyed by (specifier, phase).
Dead-code elimination is taught never to strip a deferred binding.

beats.narration: Pull request thirty-thousand-nine-seventy-five, in four ideas. defer becomes a phase keyword -- but only when it is followed by a star, so existing code is untouched.
A single phase underscore defer flag threads all the way from the parser to the engine.
The requested-modules table is re-keyed by specifier and phase, and the transpiler cache version moves with it.
And dead-code elimination is taught never to strip a deferred binding -- because that would undo the very deferral it was asked for. Small in lines; precise in every one of them.
